hero.tag: Apple Health Sync and AI Insights
hero.title: Track Your Glucose,
hero.titleHighlight: Find Foods That Work for You
hero.subtitle: SugarLite is an intelligent blood sugar management app for people living with diabetes, prediabetes, or anyone concerned about glucose health. By pairing fasting, pre-meal, and post-meal readings with meals, exercise, and insulin doses, it helps you spot your personal glucose patterns and build a diet that keeps your A1C in range.
hero.metaDescription: SugarLite is a smart blood sugar app for diabetes and prediabetes. Track glucose trends, log meals, analyze glucose index pairings, sync Apple Health, and get AI insights.

privacy.title: Privacy Policy
privacy.lastUpdated: Last updated April 2026. {appName} is committed to protecting your privacy.

sections.title: 1. Information Collection
sections.intro: We collect the following types of information to provide you with better services:
items: Personal identification information (such as name, email address, phone number)
Health data (glucose readings, medication history, medical records)
Device information (device type, operating system, app version)
Usage data (app usage frequency, feature usage patterns)

sections.title: 2. Information Usage
sections.intro: We use the collected information only for the following purposes:
items: Provide, maintain and improve application features
Personalize your user experience
Send important notifications and updates
Conduct research and data analysis
Comply with legal and regulatory requirements

sections.title: 3. Information Security
sections.content: We use industry-standard security measures to protect your personal information, including SSL encryption and regular security audits. Your health data is stored in verified data centers and accessed only by authorized personnel.

sections.title: 4. Data Sharing
sections.intro: We will not sell, rent, or otherwise provide your personal information to third parties for marketing purposes. We share data only in the following cases:
items: With your explicit consent
As required by law
For data processing with trusted service providers
To protect our legitimate interests

sections.title: 5. Your Rights
items: Access the personal data we store about you
Request correction or deletion of your data
Withdraw consent for data processing
Obtain a copy of your data
File a complaint with relevant authorities

sections.title: 6. Cookies and Tracking Technologies
sections.content: We use cookies and similar technologies to improve your app experience and analyze user behavior. You can control cookie usage through your browser settings.

sections.title: 7. Apple Health (HealthKit) Notice
sections.intro: This app supports integration with Apple Health (HealthKit). Related features are clearly labeled in the app as Apple Health Sync or similar, and health data is read or written only after you explicitly grant permission.
items: After authorization, we may read or write data related to glucose management (such as glucose records).
HealthKit data is used only for in-app display, analysis, and health management features, and is not used for advertising, marketing, or profiling.
Unless required by law or with your explicit consent, we do not sell or share your HealthKit data with third parties.
You can manage or revoke permissions at any time in the iOS Health app or in Settings > Privacy & Security > Health.

sections.title: 8. Policy Updates
sections.content: We may update this privacy policy periodically to reflect changes in technology, law, or business practices. We will notify you of any significant changes through the app.

sections.title: 9. Contact Us
sections.intro: If you have any questions about this privacy policy, please contact us at:

privacy.metaDescription: SugarLite privacy policy explains how we collect, use, store, and protect your personal info, glucose data, and Apple Health data, plus your access and deletion rights.

terms.title: Terms of Service
terms.lastUpdated: Last updated April 2026. Using {appName} means you agree to these terms.

sections.title: 1. Service Overview
sections.content: {appName} is a mobile application designed for glucose health management. This app provides glucose tracking, data analysis, trend forecasting, and health reports to help users better manage their wellness.

sections.title: 2. User Responsibilities
items: Provide true, accurate and complete personal information
Keep your account information and password confidential
Be responsible for all account activities
Comply with all applicable laws and regulations
Not interfere with or disrupt the normal operation of the app

sections.title: 3. Medical Disclaimer
sections.content: This application is NOT a medical device and cannot replace professional medical advice. The data and analysis provided are for reference only and do not constitute medical diagnosis, treatment advice, or prescriptions. Do not rely on this application for medical decisions. For any health concerns, please consult a qualified healthcare professional.

sections.title: 4. Intellectual Property
sections.content: This app and all its content (including text, images, charts, and code) are protected by copyright, trademark, and other intellectual property laws. You may download and use the app for personal use, but may not copy, modify, distribute, or publicly display any content.

sections.title: 5. Usage Restrictions
items: Use the app for any illegal purpose
Harass or harm others
Attempt to gain unauthorized access
Reverse engineer or crack the app
Use automated tools to scrape data

sections.title: 6. Disclaimer of Warranties
sections.content: This app is provided as is. We make no express or implied warranties, including but not limited to warranties of merchantability, fitness for a particular purpose, or non-infringement.

sections.title: 7. Limitation of Liability
sections.content: To the maximum extent permitted by law, we are not liable for any indirect, incidental, special, or consequential damages (including data loss or business interruption).

sections.title: 8. Apple Health (HealthKit) Terms
sections.intro: If you enable Apple Health (HealthKit) features, you agree that this app may access related health data within your authorized scope for glucose sync, trend analysis, and health management display.
items: HealthKit functionality is optional, and you decide whether to grant permission.
You may revoke permission at any time in the iOS Health app or system settings; after revocation, this app will no longer access the corresponding HealthKit data.
We do not use HealthKit data for advertising, marketing, or sale to third parties.

sections.title: 9. Modification of Terms
sections.content: We reserve the right to modify these terms at any time. Modified terms become effective immediately upon posting to the app. Your continued use of the app constitutes acceptance of the modified terms.

sections.title: 10. Account Termination
sections.content: We reserve the right to terminate your account at any time for any reason, including if you violate these terms. Upon account termination, you lose access to the app and all your stored data.

sections.title: 11. Governing Law
sections.content: These terms are governed by the laws of the People's Republic of China. Any disputes shall be resolved through friendly negotiation. If negotiation fails, disputes shall be submitted to the competent courts.

sections.title: 12. Contact Us
sections.intro: If you have any questions or comments about these Terms of Service, please contact us at:

terms.metaDescription: SugarLite terms cover service scope, user responsibilities, medical disclaimer, intellectual property, HealthKit rules, account termination, liability, and disputes.

faq.title: FAQ
faq.subtitle: Frequently asked questions about {appName}

items.a: SugarLite is a professional blood sugar monitoring, trend analysis and health management app that helps users understand glucose changes and optimize daily diet through simple logging and scientific analysis.
SugarLite provides glucose trend tracking, food logging, glucose index personalized glucose response analysis, exercise tracking, AI insights, HealthKit sync, cloud sync, and timed glucose reminders.
SugarLite is suitable for people with prediabetes or diabetes, anyone focused on glucose health, people following low-sugar diet plans, and those monitoring post-meal glucose responses.

items.q: Is my data safe with SugarLite?
items.a: SugarLite prioritizes user data privacy. After login, data is automatically synced to the cloud with encrypted transmission and storage. HealthKit data is only accessed with user authorization.

items.q: How do I use glucose index pairing analysis?
items.a: After logging pre-meal glucose, log again 2 hours post-meal. SugarLite automatically pairs these readings, calculates the change, and marks the food's impact on your glucose.
Currently SugarLite only supports iOS (iPhone). An Android version is in development and will be released soon. Subscribe to our website for release notifications.

items.q: How do I sync Apple Health data?
items.a: Enable 'Apple Health Sync' in SugarLite settings and authorize access to health data. Once authorized, your glucose records will sync bidirectionally with Apple Health.
SugarLite offers a free basic version with core features like glucose logging, trend analysis, and food tracking. Some advanced features like AI deep insights may require a subscription.

faq.metaDescription: SugarLite FAQ answers questions about glucose management, app features, glucose index pairing analysis, Apple Health sync, data privacy, and subscriptions.

guide.ctaTitle: Start Managing Your Blood Sugar Scientifically
guide.ctaDescription: Download SugarLite to log every glucose reading and discover the diet that works for you.

bloodSugar.title: Complete Blood Sugar Management Guide
bloodSugar.subtitle: From glucose basics to personalized management plans, master the science of blood sugar control.

sections.heading: What is Blood Sugar? Why Manage It?
paragraphs: Blood sugar, or blood glucose, is the primary source of energy for your body's cells. When you eat, carbohydrates in food are broken down into glucose, which enters the bloodstream and raises blood sugar levels. Insulin, a hormone produced by the pancreas, acts like a key to help glucose enter cells for use or storage.

For people with diabetes or prediabetes, insulin production is insufficient or its effectiveness is impaired, causing glucose to remain in the bloodstream at elevated concentrations. Chronically high blood sugar damages blood vessels, nerves, kidneys, eyes, and other organs, leading to serious complications. Therefore, scientific blood sugar management is the cornerstone of preventing and controlling diabetes complications.

Blood sugar management isn't just for people with diabetes. Even healthy individuals experience declining glucose regulation with age and lifestyle changes. Early monitoring and management can effectively prevent prediabetes from progressing to diabetes and reduce cardiovascular disease risk.

sections.heading: What Are Normal Blood Sugar Ranges?
paragraphs: Understanding normal blood sugar ranges is the first step in glucose management. Here are the standards recommended by the International Diabetes Federation (IDF) and the American Diabetes Association (ADA):

sections.heading: Blood Sugar Monitoring Methods and Frequency
paragraphs: Blood sugar monitoring is the foundation of glucose management. Common methods include:
bullets: Fingerstick Glucose Monitoring: Using a glucometer and test strips, this is the most common method. Suitable for daily pre- and post-meal monitoring, low cost, but requires finger pricking.
Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM): A subcutaneous sensor provides real-time glucose readings 24/7. Ideal for type 1 diabetics who need fine-tuned insulin adjustments.
HbA1c Testing: Reflects average blood sugar over the past 2-3 months, the gold standard for assessing long-term control. Recommended every 3 months.
Urine Glucose Testing: Detects glucose in urine as an indirect indicator. Less accurate than direct blood testing but simple and useful as a supplementary method.

sections.heading: Factors That Affect Blood Sugar
paragraphs: Blood sugar levels are influenced by multiple factors. Understanding them helps you better control glucose:
bullets: Diet: Carbohydrates have the most direct impact on blood sugar. Refined carbs (white rice, white bread, sugary drinks) spike glucose quickly, while complex carbs in whole grains, vegetables, and legumes raise it more gradually. Protein and fat have minimal direct impact but can indirectly affect glucose when consumed in excess.

Exercise: Regular exercise improves insulin sensitivity, helping muscle cells better utilize glucose. Both aerobic exercise (walking, swimming) and resistance training (weight lifting) benefit glucose control. Aim for at least 150 minutes of moderate-intensity exercise per week.

Stress: Mental stress triggers cortisol and adrenaline release, which raise blood sugar. Chronic stress also worsens insulin resistance. Learning stress management techniques (meditation, deep breathing, yoga) is important for glucose control.
Sleep: Insufficient or poor-quality sleep reduces insulin sensitivity and raises fasting glucose. Aim for 7-8 hours of quality sleep nightly with a consistent schedule.
Medications: Some medications (corticosteroids, thiazide diuretics) can raise blood sugar. If taking these, monitor glucose more closely.

sections.heading: The Dangers of Blood Sugar Fluctuations
paragraphs: Blood sugar fluctuations—rapid ups and downs over short periods—are harmful beyond just high glucose itself. Research shows that glucose variability causes more oxidative stress than sustained hyperglycemia, leading to vascular endothelial damage.

Major risks include: accelerating atherosclerosis and increasing cardiovascular risk; damaging the nervous system and causing peripheral neuropathy; worsening retinopathy and increasing blindness risk; affecting kidney function and accelerating diabetic nephropathy; reducing quality of life through fatigue, mood swings, and cognitive decline.

sections.heading: How to Build a Personal Blood Sugar Management Plan
paragraphs: Everyone's glucose response is unique. Building a personalized management plan is essential. Here's the scientific approach:
bullets: Record Baseline Data: Log fasting, pre-meal, and 2-hour post-meal glucose for at least 2 weeks to understand your baseline levels.
Identify Glucose Patterns: Observe daily glucose trends to find peak times and triggers.
Food Glucose Response Testing: Pair pre- and post-meal readings for common foods to record each food's glucose impact. This is the core of glucose index analysis.
Develop a Meal Plan: Based on glucose index results, prioritize glucose-friendly foods and adjust meal composition and eating order.
Set an Exercise Plan: Combine your fitness level with a regular exercise routine and observe its glucose improvement effects.
Review and Adjust Regularly: Recheck HbA1c every 3 months and adjust your management plan accordingly.

sections.heading: Using SugarLite to Log and Analyze Blood Sugar
paragraphs: SugarLite is a smart app designed for blood sugar management. It helps you:
Simple Logging: Quickly log pre- and post-meal glucose with voice input and quick tags.
Glucose Index Pairing Analysis: Automatically pairs pre- and post-meal readings, calculates glucose change, and marks food impact levels.
Trend Visualization: Generate daily, weekly, and monthly trend charts for intuitive glucose pattern viewing.
Smart Insights: Generate personalized recommendations based on multiple records, identifying your friendly foods and foods to watch.
HealthKit Sync: Seamlessly sync with Apple Health to integrate all health data.
Diet and Exercise Correlation: Log meals and exercise to analyze their combined impact on glucose.

sections.heading: Frequently Asked Questions
paragraphs: Common questions about blood sugar management:
bullets: Q: My fasting glucose is normal but post-meal is high. Should I worry? A: Yes, this is typical prediabetes called impaired glucose tolerance. Adjust your diet by reducing refined carbs and increasing fiber intake.

Q: How accurate are home glucometers? A: Home glucometers typically have an error margin within ±15%, which is acceptable. Calibrate regularly with control solution and take multiple readings at the same time for averaging.

Q: Is hypoglycemia more dangerous than hyperglycemia? A: Acute hypoglycemia is indeed more dangerous, potentially causing loss of consciousness. But chronic hyperglycemia's long-term damage is equally serious. Both require active management.

Q: Does fruit raise blood sugar? A: Fruit contains natural fructose and does raise glucose, but most fruits have moderate GI. Choose low-GI fruits (apples, pears, strawberries) and limit portions to 100-200g per serving.

bloodSugar.metaDescription: SugarLite blood sugar guide covers glucose basics, normal ranges, monitoring methods, influencing factors, and personalized management plans for better control.

diabeticDiet.title: Diabetic Diet Management Guide
diabeticDiet.subtitle: From glycemic index to the plate method, master the core methods of scientific diet management and make every meal support glucose control.

sections.heading: Basic Principles of Diabetic Diet
paragraphs: The core goal of diabetic diet management is to control post-meal blood sugar smoothly through reasonable food combinations, avoid large glucose fluctuations, and ensure balanced nutrition. This doesn't mean giving up delicious food entirely—it's about learning to choose and combine foods scientifically.

Basic principles include: controlling total calorie intake and maintaining a healthy weight; choosing low glycemic index (GI) foods; increasing dietary fiber intake; reasonably distributing calories across three meals; paying attention to eating order; limiting added sugars and saturated fats.

sections.heading: Glycemic Index (GI) and Glycemic Load (GL)
paragraphs: Glycemic Index (GI) measures how quickly carbohydrates in food raise blood sugar. GI ≤ 55 is low-GI, 56-69 is medium-GI, and ≥ 70 is high-GI.

But GI only reflects carbohydrate 'quality,' not 'quantity.' Glycemic Load (GL) considers both quality and quantity, calculated as: GL = GI × digestible carbs per serving (g) ÷ 100. GL ≤ 10 is low, 11-19 is medium, and ≥ 20 is high.
For example, watermelon has a high GI (72), but because it's mostly water, a 100g serving has GL of only 4, which is low. White rice has GI of 83, and a bowl (150g) has GL of 43, which is high.

sections.heading: Recommended Foods
paragraphs: The following foods have minimal impact on blood sugar and are suitable for daily consumption by people with diabetes:

foodLists.title: ✅ Recommended Foods
items: Whole grains: oats, brown rice, whole wheat bread, quinoa
Legumes: black beans, red beans, chickpeas, lentils
Vegetables: broccoli, spinach, celery, cucumber, tomato
Low-GI fruits: apples, pears, strawberries, blueberries, grapefruit
Quality protein: fish, chicken breast, tofu, eggs
Healthy fats: olive oil, avocado, nuts (in moderation)

foodLists.title: ⚠️ Foods to Limit
items: Refined carbs: white rice, white flour, white bread, pastries
Sugary drinks: soda, fruit juice, milk tea, sports drinks
High-sugar fruits: lychee, longan, banana, durian, grapes
Processed foods: chips, cookies, instant noodles, sausages
Fried foods: fried chicken, french fries, fried dough sticks
High-fat meats: fatty meat, pork belly, processed meat products

sections.heading: The Plate Method: How to Build Every Meal
paragraphs: The plate method is a simple, intuitive way to structure meals by dividing your plate into four sections:
bullets: 1/2 Plate: Non-starchy vegetables (broccoli, spinach, cucumber, tomato, etc.). Vegetables are rich in dietary fiber, which slows gastric emptying and reduces post-meal glucose rise.
1/4 Plate: Quality protein (fish, chicken breast, tofu, eggs, etc.). Protein increases satiety, reduces total calorie intake, and has minimal direct impact on blood sugar.
1/4 Plate: Whole grains or starchy vegetables (brown rice, oats, sweet potato, corn, etc.). Choose low-GI carbohydrate sources and control portions.
Beverages: Choose water, light tea, or unsweetened coffee. Avoid sugary drinks and juice.
sections.tip: Eating order matters too: eat vegetables first, then protein, and finally carbohydrates. This uses vegetables and fiber to delay carbohydrate absorption and lower post-meal glucose peaks.

sections.heading: Pre/Post-Meal Glucose Pairing Analysis
paragraphs: glucose index is one of SugarLite's core features. By pairing pre- and post-meal glucose readings, it helps you understand how different foods truly affect your personal blood sugar.

How it works: Measure and log pre-meal glucose (baseline) before eating, then measure again 2 hours after the meal. SugarLite automatically calculates the glucose change (Δ = post-meal - pre-meal) and marks the food's impact level based on the magnitude of change.
Δ < 2.0 mmol/L: Friendly food—prioritize these.
Δ 2.0-3.9 mmol/L: Moderate impact food—consume in moderation.
Δ ≥ 4.0 mmol/L: High-risk food—limit or avoid.
Through multiple records, SugarLite builds your personal food glucose response database and intelligently recommends dietary plans tailored to you.

sections.heading: Tips for Eating Out
paragraphs: Eating out is a common challenge for people with diabetes. These tips can help you better control blood sugar:
bullets: Plan ahead: Check the restaurant menu and choose dishes with vegetables, quality protein, and low-GI staples.
Control portions: Restaurant portions are often large. Ask for smaller portions or share with others.
Choose cooking methods: Prioritize steamed, boiled, grilled, or stewed dishes. Avoid fried and braised options.
Watch for hidden sugars: Many dishes (sweet and sour pork, fish-flavored shredded pork) contain large amounts of added sugar—choose carefully.
Adjust eating order: Even when dining out, try to eat vegetables first, then protein, and finally staples.
Carry a glucometer: Monitor blood sugar anytime when eating out and adjust your strategy accordingly.

sections.heading: Using SugarLite to Log Diet and Glucose Response
paragraphs: SugarLite's food logging feature makes blood sugar management simple and efficient:
Quick Logging: Take a photo or search the food database to log each meal with one tap.
Automatic Calculation: Built-in rich food database automatically calculates carbs, calories, and estimated GI values.
Glucose Index Pairing: Automatically links pre- and post-meal glucose records to generate personalized food glucose response reports.
Smart Recommendations: Based on your historical data, recommend recipes and food combinations suitable for you.
Trend Analysis: View the correlation between diet and blood sugar trends to discover hidden dietary patterns.

diabeticDiet.metaDescription: SugarLite diabetic diet guide explains glycemic index, glycemic load, recommended foods, the plate method, and glucose index pairing analysis for glucose-friendly meals.

stories.title: User Stories and Real Experiences
stories.subtitle: Learn how real users use SugarLite to improve their blood sugar management and find their own path to health.
stories.communityDescription: Over 500,000 users are managing their blood sugar with SugarLite. Download the app to start your glucose management journey and share your story with the community.

items.name: James
paragraphs: James was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes at age 45 during a routine NHS checkup. His GP prescribed metformin, but his HbA1c remained stubbornly high at 8.2%. He struggled with post-meal spikes, especially after his beloved Sunday roasts and pub lunches.

A colleague recommended SugarLite. Through glucose index pairing analysis, James discovered that 'wholemeal bread' spiked his glucose far more than expected, while 'basmati rice' had a surprisingly gentle impact. He also learned that eating his roast vegetables first, then the chicken, and finally the potatoes dramatically flattened his post-meal curve.

After three months of consistent logging, James's HbA1c dropped to 6.8%. His GP was impressed and reduced his medication. James now enjoys his Sunday roasts guilt-free, knowing exactly how to balance his plate.
items.results: HbA1c dropped from 8.2% to 6.8%, medication reduced by GP, post-meal spikes reduced by 60%
items.quote: SugarLite didn't tell me to stop eating the foods I love. It taught me how to eat them smarter. My Sunday roast is back on the menu!

items.name: Sarah
paragraphs: Sarah was diagnosed with gestational diabetes at 26 weeks during her second pregnancy. Her endocrinologist recommended strict carb counting, but Sarah found it overwhelming while juggling work and a toddler.

She started using SugarLite to log her meals and glucose. The glucose index analysis revealed that her morning 'healthy' granola with yogurt caused a massive spike, while plain oatmeal with chia seeds kept her perfectly stable. She also discovered that walking for 15 minutes after lunch made a noticeable difference to her afternoon readings.

Sarah maintained excellent glucose control throughout her pregnancy without needing insulin. Her baby was born at a healthy 3.4kg, and her glucose returned to normal within six weeks postpartum. She continues using SugarLite to maintain her healthy habits.
items.results: Maintained glucose targets throughout pregnancy without insulin, baby born at healthy 3.4kg
items.quote: As a working mum, I needed something simple. SugarLite took the guesswork out of gestational diabetes. I could see exactly what worked for MY body.

items.name: Michael
paragraphs: Michael, a 38-year-old software engineer, spent years living on takeout and energy drinks. His annual physical revealed a fasting glucose of 6.3 mmol/L (113 mg/dL) — prediabetes. His doctor warned him that without lifestyle changes, he would likely develop type 2 diabetes within five years.

Determined to avoid medication, Michael downloaded SugarLite. He was shocked to discover that his afternoon 'healthy' energy bar caused a bigger glucose spike than a chocolate chip cookie. His daily 3 PM crashes were directly linked to his snack choices, not just caffeine withdrawal.

Michael swapped energy bars for almonds and Greek yogurt, started cycling to work, and used SugarLite's exercise tracking to see how movement improved his readings. In two months, his fasting glucose dropped to 5.4 mmol/L (97 mg/dL), and he lost 10 pounds. His doctor called it a 'textbook reversal.'
items.results: Fasting glucose dropped from 6.3 mmol/L to 5.4 mmol/L, lost 10 lbs, doctor called it 'textbook reversal'
items.quote: I thought I was eating healthy because the packaging said so. SugarLite showed me the truth. Data doesn't lie.

items.name: Emma
items.condition: Family glucose management (father has type 2 diabetes)
paragraphs: Emma's father was diagnosed with type 2 diabetes at 62 after decades of shift work and irregular meals. As the family's primary cook, Emma took on the challenge of adapting their traditional Italian-Canadian family recipes to be diabetes-friendly.

Using SugarLite, Emma logged her father's glucose alongside her own as a control. She discovered that their beloved 'pasta Sundays' weren't the problem — it was the portion size and the lack of vegetables. Adding a large side salad and reducing pasta to one cup while increasing protein kept her father's post-meal glucose well within range.

The whole family adopted the new eating habits. Six months later, Emma's father's HbA1c fell from 7.8% to 6.4%, and the entire family lost a combined 25 pounds. Emma now shares her modified recipes in the SugarLite community.
items.results: Father's HbA1c dropped from 7.8% to 6.4%, family lost combined 25 lbs, recipes shared with community
items.quote: We didn't have to give up our family traditions. We just had to be smarter about them. SugarLite gave us the data to make it work.

stories.metaDescription: Read real SugarLite user stories about type 2 diabetes, gestational diabetes, and prediabetes. See how logging and glucose index analysis improve glucose control.
